did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes SkinViT, a transformer-based deep learning model for the automated classification of Melanoma and Nonmelanoma (Basal Cell Carcinoma and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) using dermoscopic images. The method integrates an outlooker block, transformer block, and MLP head to capture global contextual features, achieving classification accuracy on datasets derived from ISIC2019 and other dermatological databases.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning model (SkinViT) for melanoma classification, including sections on methodology, dataset acquisition, simulation setup, ablation studies, and performance results on specific datasets (ISIC2019, etc.).

title: SkinViT: A transformer based method for Melanoma and Nonmelanoma classification
brief_summary: This study proposes SkinViT, a transformer-based deep learning model for the automated binary classification of Melanoma and Nonmelanoma (Basal Cell Carcinoma and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) using dermoscopic images. The architecture integrates an outlooker block, transformer block, and MLP head to capture both fine-level and global contextual features, addressing limitations of traditional CNNs. The method was evaluated on three datasets: Dataset1 from ISIC2019, Dataset2 from various online dermatological databases, and Dataset3 combining both sources. SkinViT achieved classification accuracies of 0.9109 on Dataset1, 0.8911 on Dataset3, and 0.8611 on Dataset2. The results demonstrate that SkinViT outperforms other state-of-the-art models and previous works in literature, showing higher performance efficiency for assisting dermatologists in timely diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. Used in Dataset1 (4521 images), Dataset2 (410 images), and Dataset3 (4930 images).
Included within the 'Nonmelanoma' class. Explicitly identified as a Nonmelanoma type in the abstract and methods. Sample images provided in Fig 1(b).
Included within the 'Nonmelanoma' class. Explicitly identified as a Nonmelanoma type in the abstract and methods. Sample images provided in Fig 1(c).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: Melanoma vs. Nonmelanoma (comprising Basal Cell Carcinoma and Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automated binary classification of dermoscopic images into Melanoma and Nonmelanoma classes. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and AP. The architecture uses an MLP head with an SVM L2 kernel classifier for prediction scores.

datasets_used.name: Dataset1
datasets_used.description: A public dataset extracted from ISIC2019 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 challenge), containing dermoscopic images. The study utilized a subset of this dataset focusing on two classes.

use.training: Used for training the SkinViT model (80% split).
use.testing: Used for testing the SkinViT model (20% split).

datasets_used.name: Dataset2
datasets_used.description: A dataset collected from various online dermatological databases, specifically DermIS, PH2, and Dermnet-NZ, to provide more representation of the considered classes.

use.training: Used for training the SkinViT model (80% split).
use.testing: Used for testing the SkinViT model (20% split).

datasets_used.name: Dataset3
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset created by merging Dataset1 and Dataset2 to increase the sample size and representation.

use.training: Used for training the SkinViT model (80% split).
use.testing: Used for testing the SkinViT model (20% split).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance on individual classes by counting True Positives (Tpos), True Negatives (Tneg), False Positives (Fpos), and False Negatives (Fneg).
To assess the model's ability to distinguish between classes across various thresholds by calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To examine model performance specifically for the positive class (Melanoma) by calculating Average Precision (AP).

To determine optimal hyperparameters and components by comparing performance across different L2 kernel values, optimizers (Adam vs. RMSprop), data augmentation strategies, and classifier kernels (L1, L2, Gaussian).
To validate the efficiency of SkinViT by comparing its accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score against other models (EfficientNetV2, MaxViT, MobileViTV2, ViT) on the same datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, AUC) and percentage gains/losses relative to other models, without statistical significance testing.

Dataset1.description: Extracted from ISIC2019, 8473 total images (4521 Melanoma, 3952 Nonmelanoma). Split 80:20 for training/testing.
Dataset1.Notes: SkinViT outperformed second-best model (ViT) with a gain of 4.73% in accuracy, 0.77% in recall, 2.81% in precision, and 4.01% in F1-score.

Dataset2.description: Collected from DermIS, PH2, and Dermnet-NZ, 1082 total images (410 Melanoma, 672 Nonmelanoma). Split 80:20 for training/testing.
Dataset2.Notes: SkinViT outperformed second-best model (ViT) with a gain of 3.7% in accuracy, 10.98% in recall, and 8.17% in F1-score. Precision was lower than ViT by 2.79%.

Dataset3.description: Combined Dataset1 and Dataset2, 9554 total images (4930 Melanoma, 4624 Nonmelanoma). Split 80:20 for training/testing.
Dataset3.Notes: SkinViT outperformed second-best model (MaxViT) with a gain of 4.55% in accuracy, 9.73% in recall, and 5.35% in F1-score. Precision was lower than MaxViT by 0.59%.

HAM10000.description: External validation dataset used to compare with previously published work.
HAM10000.Notes: SkinViT achieved higher accuracy than MobileNet and STDP based SNN.

architecture_details.backbone: Transformer encoder with outlook attention mechanism
components: Outlooker block (generates fine-level features via outlook attention and MLP)
Transformer block (multi-head outlook attention, layer normalization, MLP with GeLU)
SkinViT MLP head (flatten layer, dense layer with Swish activation, SVM classifier)
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Outlook attention (computes similarity between spatial location and neighboring elements in a local window, unlike self-attention)
architecture_details.normalization: Layer Normalization (LN) added before each block in Outlooker and Transformer

classification_or_output_head.details: Uses L2 regularization to handle multicollinearity and prevent overfitting; performs best with large number of features

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: All images converted to a single size of 224 × 224 pixels to match the deep learning model input.
Data Augmentation: Applied geometric transformations on training data including 180˚ rotation, horizontal flip, and shear transformation (factor 0.2) to handle class imbalance.
Labeling: Melanoma images labeled as 1, Nonmelanoma images labeled as 0.
Data Splitting: 80:20 split rule applied for training and testing sets.
conclusions: The proposed SkinViT method, which integrates an outlooker block, transformer block, and MLP head to capture both fine-level and global contextual features, effectively addresses the limitations of traditional CNNs in capturing global information for Melanoma and Nonmelanoma classification.

SkinViT achieved classification accuracies of 0.9109 on Dataset1 (ISIC2019), 0.8911 on Dataset3 (combined), and 0.8611 on Dataset2 (online dermatological databases), demonstrating high performance efficiency.

The proposed SkinViT model outperformed state-of-the-art models (including ViT, EfficientNetV2, MaxViT, and MobileViTV2) and previous works in the literature, showing higher accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score across the tested datasets.
Ablation studies confirmed that the L2 kernel classifier with a value of 0.1, the Adam optimizer, and data augmentation strategies contributed to optimal model performance and generalization.
The study represents the first classification of Melanoma and Nonmelanoma utilizing Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) under the Nonmelanoma class.
The method is expected to assist dermatologists in the timely diagnosis and treatment of Melanoma and Nonmelanoma patients by providing an automated detection system.
important_remarks: The study claims to be the first to classify Melanoma and Nonmelanoma using Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) under the Nonmelanoma class, but this novelty claim is questionable as BCC and SCC are standard non-melanoma skin cancers.
The datasets exhibit significant class imbalance (e.g., Dataset2 has 410 Melanoma vs. 672 Nonmelanoma images), which the authors acknowledge can lead to misinterpretation of the minority class.
Data leakage risk exists because Dataset3 is a combination of Dataset1 (ISIC2019) and Dataset2 (online databases); if there is any overlap between these sources, performance metrics may be inflated.

The model was trained from scratch without pre-training, which is a limitation given the moderate dataset sizes (max 9,554 images), potentially limiting generalization compared to models using large-scale pre-training.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance gains over state-of-the-art baselines; comparisons rely solely on absolute metric values.

External validation was limited to a comparison with previous works on the HAM10000 dataset rather than a rigorous independent test set split, limiting the assessment of true external generalizability.

The paper acknowledges that geometric data augmentation (rotation, flip, shear) was used to handle imbalance but admits that advanced techniques like GANs, MixUp, or CutMix were not explored, which could further improve generalization.

The SVM classifier in the MLP head uses an L2 kernel with a specific hyperparameter (0.1) tuned via ablation studies, but the robustness of this specific configuration across different data distributions is not extensively tested.

The study focuses on binary classification (Melanoma vs. Nonmelanoma) rather than multi-class differentiation among specific non-melanoma types (BCC vs. SCC), which limits clinical utility for differential diagnosis.
